title: Workshop: Valuing Projects and Firms
summary: Unit 3 discussion: NPV, IRR, capital budgeting, stock valuation, multiples. Tie back to interactive cashflow-waterfall and DDM tools.

questions.prompt: Two mutually exclusive projects: A has NPV $100 and IRR 30%. B has NPV $1,000 and IRR 15%. Cost of capital 10%. Which does your group pick, and why?
questions.notes: B. NPV maximizes shareholder value. IRR can mislead on scale: project A could only invest a tiny amount; project B creates 10× the absolute value. Use IRR for ranking *only* when projects are scaled comparably and unconventional cash flow patterns don't reverse the signs.

questions.prompt: Sketch a cash-flow pattern with non-conventional sign changes (e.g., -100, 300, -250). What's the IRR? Why does NPV remain a reliable signal?
questions.notes: Multiple IRRs possible (the polynomial has multiple roots). NPV gives one answer at any given r. Use modified IRR or NPV decision rule directly to avoid the trap.

questions.prompt: Two analysts disagree: Analyst A uses 8% discount rate (the firm's WACC), Analyst B uses 12% (industry average for risky projects). Same FCF stream. Argue when each is right.
questions.notes: WACC if project has firm-average risk. Industry-adjusted rate if project risk differs (e.g., expanding into a riskier line). Mismatched discount rate is the most common DCF error in practice — should use *project* risk, not firm risk.

questions.prompt: A firm earns $5 EPS next year with a 10% cost of equity. It can pay everything out, or retain 40% of earnings and reinvest. Work out the share price if the reinvestment earns 15%, versus if it earns 7%. What general rule does the comparison illustrate?
questions.notes: No-growth benchmark: pay everything out, P = EPS/r_E = 5/0.10 = $50. Retain 40% (b = 0.4) so Div₁ = 5 × 0.6 = $3, and g = b × return. At a 15% return: g = 6%, P = 3/(0.10 − 0.06) = $75, so reinvestment creates $25 of value (return 15% > r_E 10%). At a 7% return: g = 2.8%, P = 3/(0.10 − 0.028) ≈ $41.67, below the benchmark, so the same growth destroys value (return 7% < r_E 10%). Rule: growth adds value only when the return on new investment exceeds the cost of equity. Growth at the wrong return destroys value.

questions.prompt: Valuation by multiples (P/E, EV/EBITDA): name three pitfalls of relative valuation. When is the method most reliable?
questions.notes: Pitfalls: (1) cherry-picking comparables, (2) accounting differences across firms, (3) industry-wide mispricing (everyone is overvalued). Most reliable: mature industries with many similar firms, stable margins, and comparable capital structures.

questions.prompt: A startup has negative FCF for years 1-5 and turns FCF-positive in year 6. Show how the terminal value typically dominates DCF in this case. What does that mean for sensitivity?
questions.notes: Terminal value at year 5 = FCF₆/(r-g) discounted back 5 periods. Often 60-80% of total PV. DCF is sensitive to terminal assumptions (r, g) more than near-term forecasts — a key risk for startup valuations.

questions.prompt: A firm returns about $15B a year in dividends and $90B in share repurchases. An analyst values its equity with a pure dividend-discount model. What does that miss, and how should the group fix the valuation?
questions.notes: A pure DDM values only the $15B dividend stream and ignores the $90B of buybacks, capturing only about a seventh of the cash actually returned ($15B of $105B) and badly understating equity. Fix: the total-payout model. Discount total payouts (dividends plus repurchases) to get total equity value, then divide by shares outstanding. Growth applies to total payouts, not to per-share dividends.
